perturbation.original_text: Recipient wishes to engage the Provider to provide certain services and resources (the "Services") and Provider desires to provide Recipient with the Services all in accordance with the terms and conditions set forth herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient wishes to engage the Provider to provide certain services and resources (the "Deliverables") and Provider desires to provide Recipient with the Services all in accordance with the terms and conditions set forth herein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Services' to 'Deliverables' in the recitals introduces ambiguity. Throughout the agreement, 'Services' is defined and used consistently. By calling it 'Deliverables' here, it creates a contradiction regarding what the Provider is actually contracted to provide. This contradiction appears in section 1, where the original definition of 'Services' is used.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Services. 1.1 Provision of Services. (a) Provider agrees to provide the Services set forth on the Exhibit A attached hereto (as such Exhibit may be amended or supplemented pursuant to the terms of this Agreement, the "Exhibit") to Recipient for the respective periods and on the other terms and conditions set forth in this Agreement and in the Exhibit. Notwithstanding the contents of the Exhibit, Provider agrees to respond in good faith to any reasonable request by Recipient for access to any additional services and resources that are necessary for the operation of the Recipient and which are not currently contemplated in the Exhibit, at a price to be agreed upon after good faith negotiations between the Parties. Any such additional services and resources so provided by Provider shall constitute Services under this Agreement and be subject in all respect to the provisions of this Agreement as if fully set forth on the Exhibit as of the date hereof. (b) Recipient may freely assign its rights under this Agreement to receive the Services to any of its affiliates.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Services' to 'Deliverables' introduces a contradiction because the agreement elsewhere, specifically in section 1, defines and uses the term 'Services'. By renaming it 'Deliverables' in the recitals, the agreement now has conflicting terminology, creating uncertainty about the subject matter of the contract and making it unclear whether the 'Services' described in section 1 are the same as the 'Deliverables' referenced in the recitals. The change introduces ambiguity regarding what the Provider is actually contracted to provide, contradicting the clear definition of 'Services' and how it is subsequently applied, as described by the definition of Misaligned Terminology and the provided Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Provider shall maintain complete and accurate records relating to the provision of the Services under this Agreement, in such form as Recipient shall approve.
perturbation.changed_text: Provider shall maintain complete and accurate records relating to the provision of the Services under this Agreement, in such form as the Regulator shall approve.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Recipient' to 'Regulator' creates confusion. The agreement consistently refers to the 'Recipient' as the party receiving the services. Introducing 'Regulator' implies an external oversight body, which isn't defined, potentially leading to disputes about whose approval is required for the records. The contradiction is located at section 2, where the Recipient is the one who makes the payments.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2 Terms of Payment and Related Matters. (a) As consideration for provision of the Services following the Effective Date, Recipient shall pay Provider an amount equal to Provider's actual cost of providing the Services plus a 10% service fee. In addition to such amount, in the event that Provider incurs reasonable and documented out-of-pocket expenses in the provision of any Service, including, without limitation, license fees and payments to third-party service providers or subcontractors (such included expenses, collectively, "Out-of-Pocket Costs"), Recipient shall reimburse Provider for all such Out-of-Pocket Costs. (b) (i) Provider shall provide Recipient with monthly invoices ("Invoices"), which shall set forth in reasonable detail, with such supporting documentation as Recipient may reasonably request with respect to Out-of-Pocket Costs, amounts payable under this Agreement, and (ii) payments pursuant to this Agreement shall be made within fifteen (15) days after the date of receipt of an Invoice by Recipient from Provider. (c) Provider shall allow the Recipient to use [ * * * ] at no cost, until December 31, 2021.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Recipient' to 'Regulator' in the original text creates a contradiction because the contradicted text (Section 2.2) clearly states that the 'Recipient' is responsible for payments and receiving invoices. Introducing 'Regulator' as the approver of records creates ambiguity and conflicts with the Recipient's defined role in the payment process. It's unclear if the Recipient's obligation is still valid since the Regulator is responsible for approving records, potentially impacting the payment approval process.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall own, and Provider hereby irrevocably assigns to the Recipient, all rights, title, and interest in any invention, technique, process, device, discovery, improvement, or know-how, whether patentable or not and all other proprietary rights, industrial rights and any other similar rights, in each case on a worldwide basis, and all copies and tangible embodiments thereof, or any part thereof, in whatever form or medium hereafter made or conceived solely or jointly by Provider while working for or on behalf of the Recipient, which relate to, is suggested by, or results from the Services.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall own, and Provider hereby irrevocably assigns to the Company, all rights, title, and interest in any invention, technique, process, device, discovery, improvement, or know-how, whether patentable or not and all other proprietary rights, industrial rights and any other similar rights, in each case on a worldwide basis, and all copies and tangible embodiments thereof, or any part thereof, in whatever form or medium hereafter made or conceived solely or jointly by Provider while working for or on behalf of the Recipient, which relate to, is suggested by, or results from the Services.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'the Recipient' to 'the Company' creates ambiguity. The agreement defines the Recipient as TELCOSTAR PTE, LTD., and Ability Computer & Software Industries Ltd. 'Company' is undefined and implies a different legal entity. This creates a contradiction in who owns the intellectual property developed. This contradiction arises in section 5, where the Recipient is indemnified.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.1 Indemnification. Provider shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Recipient and its officers, directors, employees, agents, affiliates, successors, and permitted assigns (collectively, "Indemnified Party") against any and all losses, damages, liabilities, deficiencies, claims, actions, judgments, settlements, interest, awards, penalties, fines, costs, or expenses of whatever kind, including attorneys' fees, fees and the costs of enforcing any right to indemnification under this Agreement, and the cost of pursuing any insurance providers, incurred by Indemnified Party or awarded against Indemnified Party (collectively, "Losses"), relating to/arising out of or resulting from any claim of a third party or Recipient arising out of or occurring in connection with Provider's negligence, willful misconduct, or breach of this Agreement. Provider shall not enter into any settlement without Recipient's or Indemnified Party's prior written consent.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Recipient' to 'Company' in the assignment clause directly contradicts the indemnification clause. The original text ensures that intellectual property rights are assigned to 'Recipient' (defined as TELCOSTAR PTE, LTD., and Ability Computer & Software Industries Ltd.). The changed text assigns these rights to 'Company,' which is undefined. This creates a direct conflict with Section 5.1, which indemnifies 'Recipient.' Since the 'Company' is not the 'Recipient', the indemnification clause does not directly protect the Recipient from losses, damages, liabilities, etc. if the 'Company' is at fault.
